panel.origin: The point where a player or object appears in the game world. 'Spawn point' is where you start or re-enter, and enemies 'spawn' into existence too.
panel.mixology: The team rushed out of spawn the moment the round began.
panel.examples: From 'spawn' (to produce offspring, as fish do en masse). In games, characters and items 'spawn' into being at set points, often in waves.

panel.origin: To return to the game after dying, reappearing at a spawn point. Respawn rules — timers, locations, limits — shape much of competitive play.
panel.mixology: After getting eliminated, she had to wait ten seconds to respawn and rejoin the fight.
panel.examples: From 're-' (again) + 'spawn'. The ability to respawn defines a game's stakes — instant respawns feel forgiving; permadeath makes every life precious.

panel.origin: Repetitive gameplay done to earn experience, currency, or loot. 'Grinding' is the long, often tedious path to leveling up or unlocking rewards.
panel.mixology: He spent the weekend grinding the same dungeon to finally hit max level.
panel.examples: From 'grind' (laborious, repetitive work). The gameplay loop of repeating tasks for incremental rewards — sometimes satisfying, sometimes a chore.

panel.origin: Items, gear, and currency collected from defeated enemies, chests, or the environment. 'Loot' drives much of the reward and progression in games.
panel.mixology: The boss dropped rare loot, including a legendary weapon she'd hunted for weeks.
panel.examples: From the Hindi 'lūṭ' (plunder, booty), absorbed into English via colonial India. In games it's the treasure and gear that reward exploration and combat.

panel.origin: The attention or hostility of enemies, especially AI-controlled ones. To 'pull aggro' is to make enemies target you — sometimes on purpose, sometimes disastrously.
panel.mixology: The tank pulled aggro so the enemies attacked him instead of the fragile healer.
panel.examples: A clipping of 'aggravation' or 'aggression'. It names an enemy's targeting priority — managing who holds aggro is central to team-based combat.

panel.origin: The mandatory wait time before an ability can be used again after activating it. Cooldowns force players to time their powerful moves carefully.
panel.mixology: She unleashed her ultimate, then had to survive ninety seconds of cooldown before using it again.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the ability must 'cool down' before reuse, like an overheated engine. Cooldowns balance powerful abilities by limiting how often they fire.

panel.origin: The invisible shape defining where a character or object can be hit. A shot landing within the hitbox counts; outside it misses, even if it looks close.
panel.mixology: The complaint was that the hitbox was too big, registering hits that clearly should have missed.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'box' (or shape) that registers a 'hit'. This hidden geometry determines collisions and damage, and its size is a constant source of debate.

panel.origin: FPS (frames per second) — how many images the game displays each second. Higher frame rates mean smoother motion and a competitive edge.
panel.mixology: He upgraded his rig to hit 240 frames per second for buttery-smooth, responsive aiming.
panel.examples: From film's 'frames'. A game's frame rate (confusingly also abbreviated FPS, like the genre) is prized by competitive players for smoothness and responsiveness.

panel.origin: A competitive game mode where wins and losses adjust your rank, matching you against similarly-skilled players. The ladder serious players climb.
panel.mixology: She spent the season climbing ranked, grinding from Gold up to Diamond.
panel.examples: From 'rank' (a position in a hierarchy). Ranked play sorts players into tiers and divisions, providing the structured competitive ladder for the dedicated.

panel.origin: A rating system measuring player skill, used to match opponents fairly. 'MMR' (Matchmaking Rating) is the modern hidden version behind the scenes.
panel.mixology: His Elo climbed steadily as he won, pairing him against tougher opponents each game.
panel.examples: Named for physicist Arpad Elo, who devised the system for chess. Adopted by video games as the math behind skill-based matchmaking and ranking.

panel.origin: A high-skilled player using a new or low-ranked account to play against weaker opponents. Often frowned upon for ruining matches for beginners.
panel.mixology: The lobby suspected a smurf when one player dominated far above everyone else's level.
panel.examples: Originating in 'Warcraft', where two top players used the names 'Papa Smurf' and 'Smurfette' to hide their identity. The term stuck for any expert on an alt account.

panel.origin: The dominant strategies, characters, or tactics currently considered most effective. The 'meta' shapes how everyone plays — until it shifts.
panel.mixology: After the patch, the meta shifted entirely, and yesterday's best character became useless.
panel.examples: Often explained as 'Most Effective Tactics Available', though it really comes from 'meta' (above/beyond) — the game-about-the-game of optimal strategy choices.

panel.origin: To win a high-pressure situation against the odds, often alone against multiple opponents. A 'clutch play' is the heroic, last-second victory.
panel.mixology: Down to one player, he clutched the round with three straight eliminations.
panel.examples: From the sports sense of performing 'in the clutch' (a critical moment). A clutch is gaming's most celebrated feat — snatching victory from near-certain defeat.

panel.origin: To lose a game from a winning position through mistakes or poor decisions. To 'throw' is to snatch defeat from the jaws of victory.
panel.mixology: They had it won, then threw the whole match with one greedy, reckless mistake.
panel.examples: From 'throw' a game (to deliberately lose), though in modern usage it usually means an accidental, painful collapse rather than intentional cheating.

panel.origin: 'Good game' — said at a match's end as a sign of sportsmanship. 'GG' can be sincere, sarcastic ('GG EZ'), or a concession that the game is lost.
panel.mixology: After the hard-fought match, both teams typed 'GG' in mutual respect.
panel.examples: An initialism for 'good game', a courtesy carried over from early online play. Its meaning bends with tone — gracious, dismissive, or a white-flag surrender.

panel.origin: A character built to absorb damage and protect teammates, drawing enemy fire while dealing less damage themselves. The team's frontline shield.
panel.mixology: The tank charged in first, soaking up damage while the team followed behind.
panel.examples: From military 'tanks' — heavily armored and durable. The tank role trades offense for survivability, anchoring a team by absorbing punishment.

panel.origin: Damage Per Second — both a measure of damage output and the name for damage-dealing roles. 'The DPS' are the characters who do the killing.
panel.mixology: The DPS focused the enemy healer while the tank held the line.
panel.examples: An acronym measuring 'damage per second'. It became shorthand for the damage-dealer role itself — the glass cannons who provide a team's offensive power.

panel.origin: A role focused on aiding teammates through healing, buffs, or utility rather than dealing damage. Healers keep the team alive; supports enable everyone else.
panel.mixology: A great support, she kept the whole team topped up and shielded through every fight.
panel.examples: From 'support' (to assist). These selfless roles win games quietly, healing and empowering teammates rather than chasing kills and glory themselves.

panel.origin: Multiplayer Online Battle Arena — a genre where two teams of players each control a single hero, battling to destroy the enemy's base. Hugely popular in esports.
panel.mixology: The MOBA's two teams fought lane by lane to push toward the enemy's base.
panel.examples: An acronym for the genre that grew from 'Defense of the Ancients'. MOBAs like League of Legends and Dota 2 became esports juggernauts.

panel.origin: First-Person Shooter — a genre played from the character's own viewpoint, centered on gun combat. Note: confusingly shares its initials with 'frames per second.'
panel.mixology: He preferred the FPS genre, where fast reflexes and precise aim decided every duel.
panel.examples: An acronym for the genre seen through the character's own eyes ('first-person'). It shares its letters with the unrelated frame-rate measure — context tells them apart.

panel.origin: A genre where many players fight on a shrinking map until one player or team remains. Popularized by games like PUBG and Fortnite.
panel.mixology: In the battle royale, a hundred players dropped in and the safe zone shrank until one was left.
panel.examples: Named after the Japanese film 'Battle Royale', about a last-one-standing fight. The shrinking-map, last-survivor formula became one of gaming's biggest crazes.

panel.origin: Massively Multiplayer Online — a genre where thousands of players share a persistent online world simultaneously. 'MMORPGs' add role-playing progression.
panel.mixology: The MMO's world teemed with thousands of real players adventuring side by side.
panel.examples: An acronym for the 'massively multiplayer' experience. These vast, persistent shared worlds — World of Warcraft chief among them — host whole online societies.

panel.origin: To 'buff' is to strengthen something in a game update; to 'nerf' is to weaken it. Developers buff and nerf to keep the game balanced.
panel.mixology: The patch buffed the underused character and nerfed the one everyone complained about.
panel.examples: 'Buff' from making something stronger/'buffed up'; 'nerf' from the soft, harmless Nerf toys — to 'nerf' is to make a weapon as weak as a foam dart.

panel.origin: Crowd Control — abilities that limit enemies' actions, like stuns, slows, roots, or silences. CC controls fights by taking opponents out of the action.
panel.mixology: She chained her crowd control to lock down the enemy team while her allies cleaned up.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'crowd control' — abilities that 'control' enemies by restricting their movement or actions. Landing CC at the right moment often decides team fights.

panel.origin: Damage over Time — an effect that deals damage gradually over a period, like poison or burning, rather than all at once. Steady, lingering harm.
panel.mixology: His poison DoT ticked away at the boss's health long after the initial hit.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'damage over time'. Rather than a single burst, a DoT 'ticks' repeatedly, wearing an enemy down — and forcing them to react or cleanse it.

panel.origin: Area of Effect — an attack or ability that affects everything in a region rather than a single target. AoE clears groups of enemies efficiently.
panel.mixology: He dropped an AoE spell on the clustered enemies, hitting them all at once.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'area of effect'. AoE abilities reward grouping enemies together (or punish players who clump up), shaping positioning in every fight.

panel.origin: When a chance-based effect triggers — like a weapon randomly dealing bonus damage. 'It proc'd' means the random effect activated.
panel.mixology: Her sword had a chance to proc lightning, and it triggered at the perfect moment.
panel.examples: Believed to come from 'programmed random occurrence' or simply 'process'. It names the moment a probability-based effect fires, often at a lucky instant.

panel.origin: A weapon mechanic where shots register instantly along a line, with no travel time — if your aim is on target when you fire, it hits. Common in shooters.
panel.mixology: The hitscan rifle hit instantly, so there was no need to lead moving targets.
panel.examples: A blend of 'hit' + 'scan' — the game instantly 'scans' for a target along the aim line. Contrasts with 'projectile' weapons whose shots travel and can be dodged.

panel.origin: Time to Kill — how long it takes to eliminate an opponent once you start landing hits. Low TTK games are fast and lethal; high TTK rewards sustained accuracy.
panel.mixology: The game's low TTK meant a single burst of fire could drop an enemy instantly.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'time to kill'. A core measure of a shooter's feel — low TTK is punishing and twitchy, high TTK gives more room to react and recover.

panel.origin: A new or unskilled player. Sometimes neutral (a genuine beginner), often a mild insult for someone playing badly. Spelled 'noob', 'n00b', or 'newb.'
panel.mixology: Don't worry about the mistakes — everyone's a noob when they start out.
panel.examples: A corruption of 'newbie' (a newcomer). 'Newb' tends to mean an innocent beginner, while 'noob' often implies someone unskilled who should know better.

panel.origin: Staying hidden in one spot to ambush opponents rather than moving around. An effective but widely disliked tactic, seen as cheap or cowardly.
panel.mixology: He kept camping the same corner, picking off players who wandered past.
panel.examples: From 'camping' out in one place. The tactic of lurking in a fixed position to ambush passersby is effective yet scorned as unsporting by many players.

panel.origin: To quit a game abruptly in anger after losing or making a mistake. The dramatic, frustrated exit — sometimes with a slammed keyboard.
panel.mixology: After the third loss in a row, he ragequit and shut the whole game down.
panel.examples: A compound of 'rage' + 'quit'. It names the all-too-human act of storming off mid-game in frustration — a meme as much as a behavior.

panel.origin: A 'one-shot' is an attack powerful enough to eliminate an opponent in a single hit. The dream of burst damage — and the bane of those on the receiving end.
panel.mixology: Her sniper rifle could one-shot any enemy with a clean headshot.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the target is eliminated in 'one shot'. The ultimate burst payoff, one-shots reward precision but often spark balance complaints from victims.

panel.origin: 'Pog' or 'poggers' expresses excitement or amazement at something awesome, especially in streaming chat. The reaction to an incredible play.
panel.mixology: When the clutch landed, the whole chat erupted with 'Pog!'
panel.examples: From a Twitch emote ('PogChamp') showing a surprised face. 'Pog' became streaming culture's go-to exclamation of hype and astonishment at a great moment.

panel.origin: To 'carry' is to lead your team to victory through outstanding individual play, compensating for weaker teammates. The hard-carry wins games almost single-handedly.
panel.mixology: She hard-carried the match, racking up most of the team's kills and dragging them to a win.
panel.examples: From 'carry' (to bear the load) — a dominant player 'carries' the team on their back. The term also names damage-focused roles expected to do exactly that.
